1: The valley of dry bones — the most direct resurrection text in the OT: "The hand of YHWH was upon me, and he brought me out in the Spirit of YHWH and set me down in the middle of the valley; it was full of bones." The vision of the valley full of bones is the most vivid resurrection-vision in the OT, cited across the NT to illuminate the resurrection of Christ and believers. Ephesians 2:1-6 is the direct NT application: "you were dead in your trespasses and sins... but God... made us alive together with Christ."
2: Very many bones on the surface of the valley — extremely dry. The dryness of the bones emphasizes the completeness of the death: not recently dead but long dead, all moisture and life entirely gone. Christologically, the human condition before Christ is not merely sick or weak but dead: "even when we were dead in our trespasses" (Ephesians 2:5). The absolute dryness is the precondition for the absolute miracle — resurrection from the most extreme deadness.
3: "Son of man, can these bones live?" The divine question to Ezekiel. The humanly unanswerable question — from a natural standpoint, no: the bones are beyond any human restoration. Ezekiel wisely answers: "O Lord YHWH, you know." Christologically, this is the pattern of faith: when the question is whether the dead can live, the only answer is "you know" — the acknowledgment that resurrection is in God's hands alone. The disciples at the tomb could not have answered "can this body live?" except by trusting the one who said "I am the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25).
4: YHWH commands Ezekiel to prophesy to the bones: "O dry bones, hear the word of YHWH." The prophetic word spoken to what is dead — the word that creates as it speaks. Christologically, "the hour is coming and is now here, when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God, and those who hear will live" (John 5:25). The prophetic command to dry bones is fulfilled in the Son of Man's resurrection-summons to the dead.
5: YHWH will cause breath (ruach) to enter the bones and they will live. The ruach (breath/wind/Spirit) as the agent of resurrection — the same Spirit who brooded over the waters of creation (Genesis 1:2). Christologically, the resurrection of Jesus is explicitly a Spirit-work: "he was put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit" (1 Peter 3:18); "if the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, he who raised Christ Jesus from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through his Spirit who dwells in you" (Romans 8:11).
6: Sinews on the bones, flesh covered, skin over it, breath put within — they will live and know YHWH. The step-by-step reconstitution: bones → sinews → flesh → skin → breath. The progressive resurrection stages mirror the eschatological resurrection: "the body is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory" (1 Corinthians 15:43); the perishable clothed with the imperishable (1 Corinthians 15:53-54). The end of the resurrection process — they will live and know YHWH — is the Johannine definition of eternal life: "this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God" (John 17:3).
7: Ezekiel prophesied as commanded; a rattling sound and bones came together. The word-of-prophecy as the mechanism of physical reconstitution. Christologically, "faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ" (Romans 10:17) — the same word-mechanism. The gospel proclaimed creates the spiritual life it announces, just as the prophetic word to the bones creates the physical reconstitution it describes.
8: Sinews and flesh came on the bones, skin covered them, but no breath yet. The bodily reconstitution without the breath — an almost-resurrection, not yet complete. Christologically, this stage reminds us that bodily existence without the Spirit of God is not yet resurrection-life. The disciples before Pentecost are in this stage: gathered, formed, instructed — but not yet filled with the resurrection-breath of the Spirit (John 20:22; Acts 2:1-4).
9: YHWH commands Ezekiel to prophesy to the ruach (breath/wind/Spirit) from the four winds to breathe into the slain. The command to the Spirit to come from all four directions — the cosmic comprehensiveness of the Spirit's work. Christologically, this "breath from the four winds" anticipates Pentecost: "suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the entire house where they were sitting" (Acts 2:2). The wind of the Spirit fulfills the vision of Ezekiel 37:9.
10: The breath came, they lived, and stood on their feet — an exceedingly great army. The resurrection from the valley creates an army. Christologically, the resurrection community is called to be God's "army" in the spiritual sense: "put on the whole armor of God" (Ephesians 6:11); "fight the good fight of the faith" (1 Timothy 6:12). The exceedingly great army of Ezekiel 37:10 is the resurrection-community of believers who will ultimately number as the stars of heaven (Revelation 7:9).
11: The bones are the whole house of Israel — their bones are dried, their hope lost, they are cut off. The explicit interpretation: the dry bones = Israel in exile. "Our hope is lost" and "we are cut off" — the double death-declaration of the covenant community. Christologically, this is the disciples' experience after the crucifixion: "we had hoped that he was the one to redeem Israel" (Luke 24:21). The past-tense "had hoped" is the disciples' Ezekiel 37:11 moment, answered by the resurrection morning.
12: I will open your graves and raise you from your graves: "Therefore prophesy, and say to them, Thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I will open your graves and raise you from your graves, O my people." The first explicit grave-opening language applied to Israel. Jesus quotes the grave-opening expectation in John 5:28-29: "an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out." The divine grave-opening of Ezekiel 37:12 is accomplished by the voice of the Son of Man at the general resurrection.
13: They shall know YHWH when he opens their graves and raises them. The recognition formula at resurrection — the knowledge of YHWH obtained through the experience of resurrection. Christologically, "if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins" (1 Corinthians 15:17) — the resurrection is the evidence that confirms the identity of Jesus as Lord. The raised people of Ezekiel 37:13 "know YHWH"; the risen church "knows Jesus is Lord" (Romans 10:9: "if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved").
14: YHWH's Spirit put within them — they live and are placed in their land. The Spirit as the means of resurrection-life in the covenant land. Christologically, this is the Pentecost fulfillment: the Spirit given to the resurrected community who now inhabit the new-covenant "land" — the people of God gathered from all nations. The Spirit within them (Ezekiel 36:27) produces the life that the Spirit breathed into them (Ezekiel 37:14) — the two Spirit-promises complement each other.
15: The two-sticks vision — Ezekiel takes two sticks, writes the kingdoms on them. The sign-act of the two sticks is the enacted prophecy of national reunification. Christologically, the division of the covenant community (north/south, Jew/Greek, circumcision/uncircumcision) is healed in Christ: "he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility... that he might create in himself one new man in place of the two" (Ephesians 2:14-15).
16: The stick for Judah and the stick for Joseph (Ephraim/Israel). The two sticks representing the divided kingdom. The division is historical and real — the Assyrian exile of the northern kingdom in 722 BCE. Christologically, the deeper division that Christ heals is between humanity and God: "the dividing wall of hostility" (Ephesians 2:14) is ultimately the sin-separation of Isaiah 59:2. The two-stick reunion is the visible sign of the deeper God-humanity reunion in Christ.
17: The two sticks become one in the hand of Ezekiel. The sign-act of unification — two become one in the prophet's hand. Christologically, the one body of Christ is the fulfillment: "there is one body and one Spirit... one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all" (Ephesians 4:4-6). The unity of the two sticks becomes the unity of the one new humanity in Christ.
18: When the people ask what this means, Ezekiel is ready to explain. The sign-act generates the question that allows the divine word to be received. Christologically, this is the pedagogical pattern of all prophetic signs: they create inquiry that allows revelation. Jesus uses the same pattern: his miracles generate questions ("Who then is this?" Mark 4:41) that allow fuller teaching about his identity.
19: YHWH will take the stick of Joseph and join it with Judah's stick — making them one stick. The divine first-person unification: "I will make them one." The reunification is YHWH's act, not human political achievement. Christologically, the unity of the church is similarly divine: "that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us" (John 17:21). The Ezekiel 37:19 unification is fulfilled in the trinitarian-unity prayer of John 17.
20: Hold the sticks before their eyes. The visual evidence of the sign-act maintained for the audience. Christologically, the visible signs that confirm divine word are a consistent biblical pattern. Thomas's insistence on seeing the risen Christ (John 20:25) is answered by Christ showing his hands and side: the visible evidence confirming the resurrection-word. "Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed" (John 20:29) points beyond the visible sign to the faith it generates.
21: YHWH gathering Israel from among the nations and bringing them into their land — the second mention of the new Exodus in the chapter (cf. Ezekiel 36:24). The repeated gathering promise emphasizes its certainty and its scope. Christologically, this is the universal gathering that John 11:52 describes as Jesus's ultimate purpose: "to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad." The scattered covenant people — both Jewish and gentile believers — are gathered into the one body through Christ.
22: One nation under one king: "And I will make them one nation in the land, on the mountains of Israel. And one king shall be king over them all, and they shall be no longer two nations, and no longer divided into two kingdoms." The Messianic-king who unifies the divided covenant community. Christologically, "one king shall be king over them all" = Christ as the Lord of the unified church. "There is one Lord" (Ephesians 4:5); "there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus" (1 Timothy 2:5). The one king of Ezekiel 37:22 is the risen and ascended Christ.
23: They shall not defile themselves with idols, detestable things, or transgressions — YHWH will save them from all apostasies. The purification that accompanies the reunification. Christologically, sanctification accompanies justification: "to him who loves us and has freed us from our sins by his blood" (Revelation 1:5) — the freedom from sin is integral to the new-covenant promise. The community that YHWH saves from its apostasies is the community Christ sanctifies: "Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her" (Ephesians 5:25-26).
24: David my servant shall be king: "My servant David shall be king over them, and they shall all have one shepherd." The Messianic David-king who is also the shepherd. This dual title — king and shepherd — is uniquely royal-pastoral. Christologically, Jesus is "the good shepherd" (John 10:11, 14) and "King of kings and Lord of lords" (Revelation 19:16) simultaneously. "David my servant" (cf. Jeremiah 23:5-6; Ezekiel 33:15-16) is the Messianic figure who gathers all the earlier Davidic promises into one fulfillment.
25: They shall dwell in the land forever — the servant David shall be their prince forever. The eternal duration of the Messianic kingdom. Christologically, "of his kingdom there will be no end" (Luke 1:33) — the angel's announcement to Mary directly fulfills Ezekiel 37:25. The eternal king of the eternal covenant rules the permanently gathered people in the permanent land. The "forever" (le'olam) of Ezekiel 37:25 is the eschatological eternity of Revelation 22:5: "they will reign forever and ever."
26: The eternal covenant of peace: "I will make a covenant of peace with them. It shall be an everlasting covenant with them. And I will set them in their land and multiply them, and will set my sanctuary in their midst forevermore." The brit shalom (covenant of peace) of the new covenant. Christologically, "he himself is our peace, who has made us both one" (Ephesians 2:14); "peace be with you" (John 20:19, 21, 26) — the resurrection greeting of the risen Christ who has made the covenant of peace through his blood (Colossians 1:20: "making peace by the blood of his cross").
27: My dwelling place shall be with them: "My dwelling place shall be with them, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people." The fullest expression of the covenant formula — "I will dwell with them." Christologically, this is fulfilled in stages: (1) the Incarnation (John 1:14: "the Word became flesh and dwelt [eskēnōsen = tabernacled] among us"); (2) the Spirit indwelling the church (1 Corinthians 3:16: "God's Spirit dwells in you"); (3) the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:3: "the dwelling place of God is with man"). The three stages of divine dwelling-with-humanity are all expressed in Ezekiel 37:27's promise.
28: The nations shall know that YHWH sanctifies Israel when his sanctuary is in their midst forevermore. The universal recognition of the covenant — YHWH's relationship with Israel made visible to all nations through the permanent sanctuary. Christologically, the universal recognition of YHWH through the sanctified community is the church's mission: "you shall be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth" (Acts 1:8). The permanent sanctuary in the midst = the church as the temple of the Holy Spirit bearing witness to all nations.
